title: Bonobos aren’t more peaceful than chimpanzees
html_body:
Today in Science and science: Giving land plants algal-like superpowers, the furious ancient feather trade, and more
Brought to you by From Concept to Reality: Taking Intelligent Agents into the Real World
View this email in your browser
12 March 2026
Today’s Protostar is Tanner Robison, whose work on curious plants called hornworts could help engineer crops with higher yields. But first, catch up on the latest science news, including the misconception that bonobos are less aggressive than their chimp cousins.
Archaeology  |  News from Science
Fashionable feathers
These 800-year-old parrot feathers discovered in a Peruvian tomb still show off their brilliant coloration.  George Ola
After more than 800 years, the feathered headdresses found in pre-Incan tombs along Peru’s arid coast retain their brilliant green, yellows and blues. These ornaments once bedecked the royal and elite heads of the Ychsma culture.

But where did these feathers come from? No birds native to the region are so brilliantly colored. These feathers came from parrots native to rainforests located hundreds of kilometers away. A study out this week sheds light on how the Ychsma acquired these showy headdresses: Wild parrots were caught in the Amazon and then transported, live, over the Andes in journeys that likely took weeks to complete.

Ancient DNA found in several headdress feathers shows high genetic diversity, usually a sign of wild breeding. Moreover, the feathers’ chemical makeup indicates the birds fed on fertilized maize later in life, suggesting they were kept captive by Ychsma bird handlers. The find highlights pre-Incan Andean societies’ sophisticated trading networks—and their flair for dramatic hats. “It’s extraordinary the effort people went to, to obtain these prestigious objects that didn’t have anything to do with food or subsistence,” said archaeologist Calogero Santoro.
Read the full story
Animal Behavior  |  Science ADvances
Chimps and bonobos are both aggressive—they just have different bullies and targets
Primatologist Frans de Waal once characterized bonobos as the “make love not war” ape, based on their tendency to use sexual contact to resolve conflicts. While wild chimpanzees—their close evolutionary cousins—wage wars over resources, wild bonobos spend their time much more pleasurably. But does that really mean chimpanzees are more aggressive? A team of researchers wasn’t convinced.

Indeed, past studies have provided little clarity: One found male chimps are more aggressive than male bonobos, while another found the opposite. So, the team examined the rates of aggressive behaviors—including no-contact moves like chasing another individual as well as direct aggressive contact—exhibited by 22 captive groups of chimpanzees and bonobos. Overall, the two species were equally aggressive, but they differed in both aggressors and targets. “Despite being less aggressive towards females than chimpanzee males are, bonobo males are still aggressive towards each other,” lead author Emile Bryon explained to Science Adviser. Ultimately, Bryon and colleagues found “chimpanzee males are aggressive against all, whereas all bonobos are aggressive, but target mainly the males.” Perhaps most intriguingly, the team found considerable differences between the studied groups ; both the most aggressive and least aggressive groups were bonobos.

As the two species are our closest living kin, “some see in them a reflection of our nature or how we ought to be : aggressive and war-minded like chimpanzees, or peaceful like bonobos,” said Bryon in a statement. “The truth is that, evolutionarily speaking, we are equally related to both species. And the dichotomy between aggressive chimpanzees and peaceful bonobos might be less clear than previously thought.” What studies like this really tell us about our own nature is more nuanced. “Aggression can vary widely,” Bryon told Science Adviser. “Recognizing this variation and understanding its roots suggests that it can be better understood, managed, and even reduced.”
Read the paper
SPONSORED
Wallonia: A story of biopharma growth
What does biopharma growth look like on the ground in Wallonia, Belgium? This Q&A explores how the region has built research capacity, expanded lab space, and supported companies through public investment, clinical networks, and talent development.
Read more
Protostar
Tanner Robison with a hornwort  Fay-Wei Li
Tanner Robison
Postdoctoral Scientist, Cornell University

Robison et al. An unconventional Rubisco small subunit underpins the CO2-concentrating organelle in land plants. Science 391, 1070–1075 (2026). 10.1126/science.aea0150
Tanner Robinson pays attention to things that other people tend to overlook. In college, he became fascinated by bryophytes—a group of small, non-vascular plants that include mosses and the other “little green guys” that thrive in wet environments.

As a graduate student in the lab of plant biologist Fay-Wei Li, Robinson focused on hornworts, and he soon discovered that there was far more to these unassuming organisms than meets the eye. Microscopic cavities on the hornwort’s surface, which serve as homes for nitrogen-fixing cyanobacteria, also turned out to contain a previously unknown species of green alga. The discovery occurred in the midst of the COVID-19 pandemic, prompting Robinson to suggest naming the species after poet Amanda Gorman, whose work inspired lab members with feelings of renewed hope.

But the most fascinating thing about hornworts, Robinson explains, has to do with their remarkable ability to concentrate carbon dioxide—a trait that allows them to photosynthesize more efficiently than most other plants. All photosynthetic organisms rely on Rubisco, the most abundant enzyme on Earth, to convert carbon dioxide to sugar. But even though Rubisco is essential to the process of photosynthesis, it isn’t particularly efficient. Many species of algae get around this problem using specialized compartments called pyrenoids, which are chock-full of Rubisco and saturated with concentrated carbon dioxide.

Scientists have long been intrigued by the idea of engineering pyrenoids into crops like rice and wheat to increase agricultural yields. It has proven difficult, however, to insert molecular machinery from algae into land plants. That, Robinson explains, is where hornworts come in: They are the only land plants that have evolved pyrenoids. In fact, for reasons that remain mysterious, they have evolved and lost these photosynthesis-boosting structures at least five times in the past 100 million years.

Now, in a new Science study, Robinson and his colleagues have finally figured out how hornworts assemble their pyrenoids. As plant scientists Moritz Meyer and Howard Griffiths explain in a related Science Perspective, it all comes down to a “ Rubisco-Rubisco handshake.”

ScienceAdviser sat down with Robinson to talk about the work. Below is that conversation, edited for brevity.

Why are pyrenoids so important?
Rubisco is considered a flawed enzyme. The first big flaw is that it only catalyzes between three and 10 reactions per second. That doesn’t sound so bad, but when you compare it to something like carbonic anhydrase, which is one of the speedier guys out there and catalyzes about a million reactions per second, it’s definitely slow.

The other problem is that Rubisco actually has a hard time distinguishing between O2 and CO2. When it reacts with O2, it produces a type of sugar that can’t be used by the plant and has to be recycled. That recycling process requires energy and also results in the loss of previously fixed CO2.

In environments where CO2 is especially limited, you often find organisms that have evolved some sort of carbon-concentrating mechanism. Virtually everything in the ocean—all the cyanobacteria and eukaryotic algae—has pyrenoids, for example, because CO2 diffuses really slowly in water.

How does this process work in hornworts?
For these CO2-concentrating mechanisms to work, you need two coordinated processes: the delivery of the CO2, and the assembly of the pyrenoid. This paper looked at the assembly of the pyrenoid.

In algae, there’s an intrinsically disordered protein. It’s like a spaghetti noodle, and it’s got all these sticky bits on it that specifically bind to Rubisco. When I first started looking for the linker protein in hornworts, that’s what I was looking for. But what I kept finding was that there were no intrinsically disordered proteins that were plausible candidates for the job, so we kind of had to go back to the drawing board.

Rubisco is composed of eight large subunits and eight small subunits. The large subunits form a kind of barrel shape, which is where the action happens. That’s where the actual carbon fixation is occurring. The small subunits are kind of like caps on either end of the barrel, and they stabilize the complex. These small subunits are deeply conserved proteins, and they pretty much stay the same size across species. So, when I looked at hornworts, I was sort of gobsmacked to see this weird extra hundred amino acids. I thought it was quite striking.

To make a long story short, that is the linker. If you think of Rubisco as a barrel, coming off the top of that barrel is this hand, which is reaching out and grabbing the neighboring Rubisco. That’s how they’re linked together.

What are the potential implications of these findings?
Previously, there have been all these attempts to take what’s happening in algae and put it in plants, but that’s trying to cross a pretty wide divide. Scientists have succeeded in introducing an algal linker into a vascular plant, but in order to do that, they had to modify the native Rubisco small subunit to introduce a binding site.

What separates the hornwort linker is that it’s not binding to Rubisco at all. It’s binding to itself. From an engineering perspective, this simplifies things. You don’t have to worry about whether this linker is going to be compatible with wheat or rice Rubisco. It’s just going to bind to its partner.

We have to see where the rubber meets the road, but the modeling says that, depending on the crop species, you could see an 80% gain in daily carbon fixation. Only time will tell how that actually shakes out, but if things work as modeled, that could translate into a lot more yield per hectare. We’ll see. Life is complicated, biology is complicated, but the promise is there.
Read the related Perspective
Read the paper
Et Cetera
Unmemorable microbe
A gut microbe more common in older mice produces compounds that impair neuronal signaling between the gut and brain, dampening activity in brain regions associated with learning and memory. The findings implicate the microbiome in age-related memory loss. The study is a “tour de force,” one neuroscientist said. “They define the pathway all the way from aging and bacteria … to cognitive function—it’s really impressive.” Though it remains unclear if a similar mechanism occurs in humans.
Nature Paper  |  Read more at News from Science
Viva Viagra
The erectile dysfunction drug Viagra can help repair abnormalities in cells with a genetic variant associated with Leigh syndrome, a rare disorder with no approved therapies that is usually fatal before the age of three. The drug also modestly extended life span among rodents and pigs bearing other disease-linked mutations. The findings suggest Viagra could be a “really promising compound … for one of the most devastating childhood rare diseases,” one expert said.
Cell Paper  |  Read more at News from Science
Darwin’s snails
The Partula snails of French Polynesia are as diverse and beautiful as they are threatened. Dedicated scientists have spent three and a half decades trying to keep the snails from going extinct—and learn about them in the process. “They’re the Darwin’s finches of the snail world,” one museum curator noted. “They have been under scientific scrutiny for more than a century, offering a unique, real-time look at how isolation drives evolution.”
Read more at The New York Times
[Permanent daylight time is] scientifically not a good idea, and in terms of health.
—Jamie Zeitzer, Stanford University
CBC News  |  5 March 2026  |  Tara Carman
Last but not least
Reading about how some frogs’ sweet serenades may relay information about their quality as a suitor and the suitability of his habitat for laying eggs makes me wish all songs conveyed such rich information. Or do they?
Christie Wilcox, Editor, ScienceAdviser

With contributions from Michael Price and Phie Jacobs

male chimps are more aggressive than male bonobos
naming the species after poet Amanda Gorman
some frogs’ sweet serenades may relay information about their quality as a suitor and the suitability of his habitat for laying eggs

raw_metadata.subject: Bonobos aren’t more peaceful than chimpanzees
